On the Contract Date, the no-lapse contract fund equals the no-lapse net premium amount credited on that date.
On the Contract Date, any charges described below may be deducted from the no-lapse net premium amount.
On each day after the Contract Date, the no-lapse contract fund is adjusted by adding any no-lapse net premium amounts.
On each day after the Contract Date, no-lapse interest is added to the portion of the no-lapse contract fund that exceeds the loan amount.
The no-lapse interest on the portion of the fund exceeding the loan amount is calculated at a rate of 1.00 per contract year from year 63 to 89.
On each day after the Contract Date, no-lapse interest is added on the portion of the fund equal to the loan amount at an effective annual rate of 3% (0.00809863% per day).
On each day after the Contract Date, the no-lapse contract fund is reduced by any withdrawals.
On each monthly date, the no-lapse contract fund is reduced by a monthly no-lapse charge of $0.10 per $1,000 of the basic insurance amount plus $10.00.
Starting on July 28, 2033, the monthly no-lapse charge for administrative expenses changes to $0.00 per $1,000 of the basic insurance amount plus $10.00 thereafter.

The S&P 500 Uncapped Indexed Account uses an annual point-to-point indexing method.

The participation rate for the account is guaranteed at 20%.
The segment start date is the date money is transferred into the indexed account from the fixed account.
The segment duration is one year from the segment start date.
The segment maturity date is one year after the segment start date.
The average daily segment value is calculated based on the daily values during the segment period.
Index interest is determined by multiplying the average daily segment value by the index growth rate.

The index growth rate is calculated as (Index value on segment maturity date / Index value on segment start date) minus 1, then multiplied by the participation rate, but not less than the index growth floor.

This contract has a Type A, Type B, or Type C death benefit.

If the contract has a Type A death benefit, the no-lapse death benefit equals the greater of the basic insurance amount or the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any no-lapse monthly charges multiplied by the attained age factor.

If the contract has a Type B death benefit, the no-lapse death benefit equals the greater of (1) the basic insurance amount plus the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any no-lapse monthly charges, or (2) the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any no-lapse monthly charges multiplied by the attained age factor.

If the contract has a Type C death benefit, the no-lapse death benefit equals the greater of (1) the basic insurance amount plus the lesser of (a) total premiums paid minus total withdrawals, or (b) the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any monthly charge plus the product of the Type C Limiting Amount and the Type C Death Benefit Factor, or (2) the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any monthly charges multiplied by the attained age factor.
For the purpose of determining the Type C no-lapse death benefit, total premiums paid do not include any charge to reinstate the contract.

When buying life insurance, you want a policy that fits your needs without costing too much.
Your first step in buying life insurance is to decide how much you need, how much you can afford to pay, and the kind of policy you want.
You should compare what various companies charge for the same kind of life insurance policy.
Life insurance cost indexes can reveal important differences in the cost of life insurance.
A good life insurance agent or company will help you with each step of shopping for life insurance.
Understanding the different kinds of life insurance available is essential for making a good choice.
If one type of life insurance does not fit your needs, you should ask about other types described in the guide.
If you need more information than provided, you may consult a life insurance agent, company, or books in your public library.
One way to decide how much life insurance you need is to calculate how much cash and income your dependents would need if you died.
Life insurance can provide cash for final illness expenses, taxes, mortgages, or other debts.
Life insurance can also provide income for family living expenses, educational costs, and other future expenses.
Your new policy should aim to make up the difference between what your dependents would have if you died now and what they would actually need.
All life insurance policies agree to pay a sum of money if you die.
Not all life insurance policies are the same.
There are three basic kinds of life insurance: term insurance, whole life insurance, and endowment insurance.
No matter how fancy the policy title or sales presentation, all life insurance policies contain one or more of the three basic kinds.
If a policy seems complicated, you should ask the agent or company if it combines more than one kind of life insurance.

If the contract has a Type B death benefit, the death benefit is the greater of (1) the sum of the basic insurance amount and the greater of the contract fund and the alternate contract fund before monthly charge deductions, or (2) the product of the attained age factor and the greater of the contract fund and the alternate contract fund before monthly charge deductions.

Term insurance provides death protection for a specific term of one or more years.
Death benefits from term insurance are paid only if the insured dies within the specified term.
Term insurance generally offers the largest immediate death protection for the premium paid.
Some term insurance policies are renewable for additional terms even if health has deteriorated.
Premiums for renewed term insurance policies increase with each renewal.
Some term insurance policies are convertible into whole life or endowment policies before the end of the conversion period.
Conversion to a whole life or endowment policy is possible even if the insured is not in good health.
Premiums for the new policy after conversion are higher than the original term insurance premiums.
Whole life insurance policies develop cash values over time if premiums are paid.
Cash values in whole life insurance can be accessed if premiums are stopped, providing nonforfeiture benefits.
Nonforfeiture benefits include the option to take cash or use the value to buy continuing insurance protection.
The amount of nonforfeiture benefits depends on the policy type, size, and duration of ownership.
Whole life insurance provides death protection for the entire life of the insured.
Straight life or ordinary life insurance has level premiums for life, which are higher than initial term insurance premiums.
Level premiums for ordinary life insurance are typically lower than the premiums for renewing term insurance in later years.
Some whole life policies allow premium payments for a limited period, such as 20 years or until age 65.
Premiums for limited-payment whole life policies are higher than for ordinary life insurance due to the compressed payment period.
A whole life policy with cash values can be used as collateral for a loan.
Interest rates on policy loans are specified in the policy document.
Outstanding policy loans may be deducted from death benefits or cash values if the insured dies or stops paying premiums.

This contract is designed to satisfy the definition of life insurance for Federal income tax purposes under Section 7702 of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended.
The insurer reserves the right to decline any change that would cause the contract to fail to qualify as life insurance under applicable tax law.
Changes to the basic insurance amount, withdrawals, and the type of death benefit could cause the contract to fail to qualify as life insurance.
The insurer has the right to change the contract, require additional premium payments, or make distributions to maintain the contract’s qualification as life insurance.

The insurer reserves the right to take necessary actions to prevent the contract from becoming a modified endowment contract under Section 7702A unless the policyholder has indicated in writing a desire to have it become one.
Monthly charges from the contract fund and alternate contract fund are discontinued on the first contract anniversary on or following the 121st birthday of the younger insured.
After the younger insured reaches age 121, the contract may continue to operate under its provisions, including Death Benefit and Contract Fund terms.
No premium payments may be made after age 121 except those required to keep the policy in force during the grace period.
Loans, loan repayments, and withdrawals may continue after the younger insured turns 121.
Cash value default cannot occur on or after the first contract anniversary following the younger insured’s 121st birthday.
Excess contract debt default may occur if contract debt equals or exceeds the cash value.
The contract may not qualify as life insurance under Federal tax law after an insured attains age 100.
Adverse tax consequences may result if the contract is continued after an insured reaches age 100.
A tax advisor should be consulted before deciding to continue the contract after an insured reaches age 100.
On each segment maturity date, index interest is credited to an indexed account segment.
After being credited to an indexed account segment, amounts are transferred to the fixed account.
If funds are allocated to an indexed account, transfers occur from the fixed account to a new indexed account segment on the same day.
Transfers from the fixed account to an indexed account are restricted during a 12-month transfer restriction period.
A transfer restriction period begins when any portion of a loan causes a reduction in an indexed account segment.
During a transfer restriction period, no transfers are allowed from the fixed account to an indexed account.
Net premiums and loan repayments received during a transfer restriction period remain in the fixed account.
After a transfer restriction period ends, amounts in the fixed account are transferred to indexed accounts if they are included in current allocation instructions.

When a beneficiary is designated, their relationship is to the Insured(s) unless otherwise stated.
Beneficiary priority is shown using numbered classes, with Class 1 having first priority and Class 2 having next priority.
If no one in a prior class survives the second of the Insureds to die, the right to be paid passes to the next class.
If only one person in a class survives the second of the Insureds to die, that person will be the only one paid.
If two or more individuals in the same class survive the second of the Insureds to die, they will be paid in equal shares.
If no one survives the second of the Insureds to die, the payment will be made in one sum to the Insured's estate.
Before making a payment, the insurer has the right to determine the required proof of identity, age, or other facts about beneficiaries.
If beneficiaries are not named and payment is made based on proof, the insurer is not required to make the payment again.

The insurer may require the contract to be sent before processing a change in beneficiary.
The insurer will file and record the beneficiary change at the Home Office upon receipt of the request and contract if required.
Unless a different effective date is specified, a beneficiary change takes effect on the date the request was signed.
Rights created by a beneficiary request do not apply to payments made or actions taken before the request was received and recorded.
If an effective date is chosen after the request was received and recorded, the rights do not apply to prior payments or actions.
A beneficiary's interest is subject to the rights of any assignee the insurer knows about.
